JavaScript Core Concepts (8): How Memory Gets Allocated and Freed (Garbage Collection)
JavaScript
Execution Context & Scope

Let's talk about how JavaScript reclaims memory. This post covers what garbage collection actually is, why older languages made you manage memory by hand, the rule JavaScript uses to decide when an object is safe to collect, and a hands-on Chrome experiment to watch memory get allocated and freed in real time.

JavaScript Core Concepts (7): not defined VS undefined — What's the Difference?
Execution Context and Scope

not defined and undefined are two really important concepts in JavaScript, and they trip people up all the time. Once you understand the difference between them, debugging gets a whole lot easier. This post walks through what each one actually means, and why you should never use undefined as a value or a variable name.

JavaScript Core Concepts (6): Hoisting — Why You Can Use a Variable Before You Declare It

Hoisting is honestly one of my favorite JavaScript concepts. Every variable and every function in JavaScript has it. In this post we'll dig into why you can use a variable before it's even declared, why you get undefined instead of an error, and why functions get higher priority than variables. Plus a little quiz at the end to test yourself.

JavaScript Core Concepts (5): Execution Context, Scope, and the Scope Chain

Why can a variable declared in an outer layer — or even a function defined somewhere else entirely — still get called and run correctly? This post breaks down the scope chain: how JavaScript walks outward to find variables and functions when the current function doesn't have them, and why this has nothing to do with the call stack.

JavaScript Core Concepts (4): Execution Context and the Call Stack

If you don't have a solid grasp of execution context and the call stack, things get confusing fast once you start building real stuff. This post walks through what an execution context actually is, how each function gets its own, and how the call stack pushes and pops as your code runs.
/en/javascript/2020/04/12/javascript-core-03-lexical-scope/
JavaScript Core Concepts (3): Lexical Scope — Where You Declare It Is Where It Lives

JavaScript uses lexical scope, which means a variable's scope is decided the moment you declare it — not when the code runs. This post breaks down what lexical scope is, the difference between static and dynamic scope, and why JavaScript falls into the static-scope camp alongside languages like C, Python, and Java.
/en/javascript/2020/04/05/javascript-core-02-lhs-rhs-errors/
JavaScript Core Concepts (2): LHS vs RHS — What Those Errors Actually Mean

You'll run into LHS and RHS all over JavaScript — they're tied to assigning values and looking values up. This post breaks down what LHS and RHS mean, when each one throws an error, and why the exact wording of those errors changes from browser to browser.

JavaScript Core Concepts (1): How JavaScript Actually Runs

This is the first post in a series on JavaScript core concepts. We kick things off with how JavaScript actually runs under the hood — what an interpreted language is, how it differs from a compiled one, and what the interpreter does step by step: tokenizing, building the AST, and finally generating code.
